That's what being a good neighbor truly means—showing up consistently and contributing meaningfully to the communities where we live and work.\"This year's Night Market overlapped with the Mid-Autumn Moon Festival, a 3,000-year-old tradition celebrated across Asia that marks the end of the summer harvest. The Seattle celebration made its debut in August 2006 as a small gathering organized by the youth leadership group WILD (Wilderness Inner-city Leadership Development) to bring residents, visitors, and vendors into the neighborhood as part of National Night Out. In 2023, the Night Market was canceled due to a lack of sponsorship. The following year, Amazon stepped in with a $250,000 three-year commitment as the lead sponsor.\"The Night Market is a great event because it shows what the neighborhood could look like at its peak,\" said Tuyen Than, executive director of the Chinatown-International District Business Improvement Area. \"And that's what we're going for: a Night Market, but all throughout the year.\" Amazon celebrates grand opening of Rainier Valley Food Bank's new facility The Rainier Valley Food Bank celebrated the grand opening of its newly renovated facility this month, welcoming hundreds of community members to the expanded space. Amazon contributed $1 million to the food bank's capital campaign, helping support its transformation from a small 1,200-square-foot location into a nearly 12,000-square-foot resource center.\"Amazon's generous contribution to Rainier Valley Food Bank directly addresses one of our city's most pressing challenges—ensuring all Seattle residents have access to affordable, nutritious food,\" said Seattle Mayor Bruce Harrell. \"As we continue efforts to expand healthy food access, we recognize this investment doesn't just expand a building - it strengthens a vital community resource that provides dignity, nutrition, and hope for thousands of South Seattle residents.\"Amazon's contribution reflects the company's commitment to addressing food insecurity in its hometown communities. Using the same delivery network that serves our retail customers, Amazon partners with food banks like Rainier Valley Food Bank to bring groceries directly to the doorsteps of families in need for free. Since 2020, Amazon has delivered more than 50 million meals across the US and UK, including 4.5 million meals in the Puget Sound region alone.The renovation of Rainier Valley Food Bank not only expands its footprint, allowing for more space to help people in need, but it also creates an elevated shopping experience and offers services beyond only food distribution. This includes:A grocery-store-style shopping area with refrigerated sections, fresh produce, and essential itemsA commercial kitchen for preparing ready-to-go lunches and hot mealsA dedicated resource center providing housing assistance and employment informationOutdoor courtyard and garden space for community gatheringsThe new facility is now open providing essential services to South Seattle residents in need. Learn more about Amazon's commitment to food security and how we're helping fight hunger in our communities. Amazon housing fund supports groundbreaking of Mary's Place and Mercy Housing Northwest family shelter and af With support from Amazon, Mary's Place and Mercy Housing Northwest broke ground today on a project that will combine an emergency family shelter with permanent affordable housing. The campus will provide shelter and comprehensive services for 200 family members alongside 90 units of affordable housing, including 34 units designated for families transitioning from homelessness to stable housing.The Burien campus represents a new approach to addressing family homelessness by creating a seamless pathway from crisis to stability. Families will benefit from wraparound services including health resources, educational support, and economic empowerment programs as well as connections to schools and community networks.\"Amazon has a long history of support for Mary's Place, and we're humbled to support both Mary's Place and Mercy Housing Northwest in this project. Public-private partnerships are essential as we tackle complex challenges, including homelessness,\" said David Zapolsky, chief global affairs and legal officer at Amazon. \"Amazon is proud to support innovative solutions that make a tangible difference in our communities. This project represents a significant step forward in our collective efforts to ensure every family has a safe place to call home.\"Governor Bob Ferguson emphasized the collaborative nature of the initiative: \"Housing is too expensive in Washington, and there is not enough of it. For many Washingtonians, this campus will be a path from crisis to stability—and a place to call home.\"When the project is completed in 2027, the campus will stand as a model, here and elsewhere, for how communities can address family homelessness.\"This project represents a fundamental shift in how we approach family homelessness,\" said Dominique Alex, chief executive officer of Mary's Place. \"By collocating emergency shelter with affordable housing, we're creating a true continuum of care that allows families to rebuild their lives without the disruption of moving to unfamiliar neighborhoods. This model recognizes that stable housing is critical, and it's exactly what families tell us they need most. We are deeply grateful for the tremendous support from so many partners, funders, and advocates who have helped us reach this milestone.\" Celebrating 10,000 affordable homes: Partnerships driving housing impact across Puget Sound When Amazon announced our housing commitments in 2021, we set ambitious goals to address the affordable housing crisis in our hometown communities. Today, thanks to collaboration with community organizations, public officials, and mission-aligned developers, we've reached a significant milestone: more than 10,000 affordable homes created or preserved across the Puget Sound region. What makes these investments truly special is our commitment to ensuring these properties remain affordable for 99 years—securing housing stability for generations to come.This achievement represents over $900 million in housing investments in the Puget Sound, benefitting 22,000 residents. Behind these numbers are families with access to stable, quality housing near jobs, schools, and transportation.\"When I think about these affordable homes, I see families finding stability and new opportunities,\" said Alice Shobe, director of community impact at Amazon. \"I'm grateful to celebrate this milestone with our amazing housing partners, passionate community advocates, and the local officials who've championed these efforts. These are solutions that will make a difference for generations to come.\"These 10,000 homes represent meaningful collaborations across the Puget Sound region. From our $100 million commitment to transit-oriented housing in Bellevue, to partnering with BRIDGE Housing to preserve 200 affordable apartments in Kirkland, each project responds to unique local needs. The Four Amigos Beloved Community in Seattle's Columbia City neighborhood offers multi-generational families spacious homes near light rail, with the newly-opened Connection Angle Lake with Mercy Housing Northwest providing 130 energy-efficient homes.\"Amazon's partnership has been transformative for affordable housing development in the Puget Sound region. Their approach combines critical financial resources with a genuine commitment to long-term community stability through 99-year affordability requirements,\" said Joe Thompson, president, Mercy Housing Northwest. \"At Connection Angle Lake, this partnership allowed us to create not just housing, but a community where residents can thrive near transit, jobs, and services.\"Amazon is energized by what these partnerships have accomplished and are actively seeking additional opportunities to collaborate. Amazon's housing fund is currently accepting applications for Puget Sound projects through September 30. Prime Video, Seattle Seahawks, and Smithsonian team up to spark a love of reading in Seattle youth Prime Video, the Seattle Seahawks, and the Smithsonian Institution brought their \"TNF for Good\" literacy initiative to Boys & Girls Clubs of King County's Ballard location.More than 125 kids were helped by Amazon volunteers to choose from a \"lawn library\" of over 1,300 books that were on display at the event. Those who attended designed bookmarks, engaged with Seattle Seahawks Legend Ben Obomanu, and took photos with Seahawks mascot Blitz.The Seattle event represents one stop in the nationwide Thursday Night Football collaboration between Prime Video, Boys & Girls Clubs of America, and the Smithsonian that aims to donate 1 million books to youth across all NFL markets in 2025. The effort reflects the partners' shared commitment to promoting literacy and fostering a love of reading among young people.\"Having a partner like the Smithsonian curate a collection of books rich in diverse stories and perspectives reflects the vibrancy of our 32 Clubs. At Boys & Girls Clubs, we use these stories to spark a love of reading that fuels imagination, and when a young person's imagination is ignited, it opens the door to a thriving, full life,\" Boys & Girls Clubs of King County CEO Lori Bae said. \"We're especially grateful to the Seattle Seahawks, who go beyond the game by sending their support with legends, dancers, and Blitz to inspire and encourage our youth. And to Amazon, who consistently shows up to ensure every young person has access to the tools, experiences, and support they need to thrive.\"\"There's something truly magical about watching a child's face light up when they find a book that speaks to them,\" said Kevin Burkhart, board member of Boys & Girls Clubs of King County and director of Echo Product & Marketing at Amazon. \"Being here today reminds me why this work matters so much. These moments create lasting impact that will extend far beyond today.\" Amazon and Ascend Hospitality Group support 800 Bellevue students with back-to-school event In celebration of the new school year, Amazon and Ascend Hospitality Group (AHG) teamed up for the fifth year of \"Ascend Has Your Back,\" providing backpacks to 800 local students in need. Students enjoyed an afternoon at a \"Summer of Soccer\" party at Amazon Fresh in Bellevue, where they received backpacks for school, personalized their packs with school supplies and fun keychains, played soccer-influenced games, met members of the Eastside's new pre-professional soccer club Midlakes United, and enjoyed lunch courtesy of Amazon. Amazon's Chief Global Affairs and Legal Officer David Zapolsky joined Bellevue Mayor Lynne Robinson, Bellevue Deputy Mayor Mo Malakoutian, and AHG Co-Founder and CEO Elaina Morris at the event. Five nonprofit partners—Boys & Girls Club of Bellevue, Rainier Athletes, Bellevue Family YMCA, Bellevue Lifespring, and 4 Tomorrow—also participated alongside dozens of Amazon volunteers to help students prepare for the school year. Amazon Fresh provided hundreds of healthy snacks for students to take home. \"Education is a huge part of what we care about, and we want to do what we can to ensure access to education for all students in our communities,\" said David Zapolsky, Chief Global Affairs and Legal Officer at Amazon. \"In 2017, we started Amazon Future Engineer, and we've been able to reach more than 2 million students around the world, including students in Bellevue, with everything from elementary school exposure to STEM to post-secondary scholarships. I continue to be inspired by our community's efforts to support students.\" Bumbershoot Gives Back, powered by Amazon, turns music passion into community action Amazon and Bumbershoot kicked off their second annual Bumbershoot Gives Back, powered by Amazon, campaign at Rainier Beach Urban Farm and Wetlands. Volunteers gathered to harvest crops in support of food security efforts across the Puget Sound and in return, receive a free ticket to Bumbershoot. The initiative builds on last year's success, which resulted in 127,000 pounds of food collected for local food banks. This year, the program aims to mobilize more than 2,000 community volunteers to support food banks in delivering 15,000 meals. As a thank you, each volunteer will receive a free two-day festival ticket. It's a powerful incentive to address one of our region's most pressing challenges—one in 10 Washingtonians faces food insecurity. \"Seattle's arts and music scene is core to this city's identity and an integral part of our cultural fabric,\" said David Zapolsky, Amazon chief global affairs and legal officer. \"We're extending Bumbershoot Gives Back, powered by Amazon, mobilizing 2,000 volunteers and employees to provide 15,000 meals to those in need across 14 food banks in the Puget Sound. This initiative reflects how the arts can be a catalyst for addressing critical needs in our communities, and Amazon's commitment to supporting local traditions and the food security of our neighbors. When we combine Seattle's creative spirit with direct community action, we can transform the joy of music into meaningful impact for those who need it most.\" Since stepping in as a title sponsor in 2023 following a four-year hiatus, Amazon has helped revitalize Bumbershoot, making it more accessible through reduced ticket pricing and providing thousands of free tickets to underserved communities. The festival now welcomes approximately 40,000 attendees over Labor Day weekend, August 30-31. Learn more and sign up to volunteer. Amazon employees beautify Puget Sound communities during Global Month of Volunteering As part of Amazon's fourth annual Global Month of Volunteering (GMV), more than 7,600 employees volunteered their time at more than 580 different events across the Puget Sound. From downtown Seattle to Bellevue and places in between, teams of employees worked with more than 220 community organizations to clean up local parks, pack essential supplies, and prepare and serve food.In Bellevue, Amazonians participated in the City of Bellevue’s \"Keep Bellevue Beautiful\" initiative. Volunteers fanned out across downtown Bellevue, collecting trash and debris to enhance the city’s shared spaces.“We are grateful for the opportunity to be part of the 'Keep Bellevue Beautiful' initiative,” said Beryl Tomay, VP of Amazon Transportation. “Our volunteers' efforts reflect Amazon's commitment to the communities where we live and work.”These park and neighborhood cleanups represent just a fraction of GMV's impact. From joining Backpack Brigade to pack weekend food bags for Seattle students who rely on school meals, to hosting mock interviews and resume building sessions for adults in need of work, employees worldwide volunteered alongside community organizations where support is needed most.Amazon’s community engagement extends beyond volunteer hours. We're working side-by-side with community partners to increase access to affordable housing, alleviate hunger, strengthen education, and help those impacted by natural disasters when they occur. Since 2021, Amazon has donated more than $244 million to local charities and nonprofit organizations across the region, while investing $720 million through Amazon's housing fund to preserve and create over 9,900 affordable homes throughout Puget Sound. AWS, Seattle Seahawks, and Play Like a Girl' team up to inspire next generation of STEM leaders When 100 middle and high school girls from across the Seattle area arrived at the Virginia Mason Athletic Center this month, they found more than just the Seahawks' practice facility–they discovered a world where sports and technology seamlessly intersect.Tech Touchdown, a collaboration between Amazon Web Services (AWS), the Seattle Seahawks, and nonprofit Play Like a Girl, transformed the Seahawks training center in Renton into an interactive learning lab where young athletes explored how cloud technology powers modern sports experiences.\"It was an awesome and inspiring day at the Seahawks Training Facility,\" said Julia White, AWS chief marketing officer. \"Helping these talented young women see themselves in both sports and STEM was incredibly rewarding.”Participants rotated through three AWS-powered stations. At My Cause My Cleats, students used generative AI to design custom cleats similar to those NFL players wear for charitable causes, each taking home a custom design keepsake. The NFL Football Throw station tested quarterback abilities with sensor-equipped footballs that feature the same technology used to track throws by NFL players. Finally, at a Seahawks Hologram station, participants could “interact” with former Seahawks wide receiver Ben Obomanu and see themselves transformed into holograms.The day also featured appearances by current Seahawks’ players, including wide receiver Dareke Young and linebacker Boye Mafe who participated in flag football drills alongside students.\"We're going to show him how it's done!\" one student declared before showcasing her athleticism and taking the flag. Breaking Ground on Acer House: Amazon-backed project brings bold vision to Seattle’s Central District David Zapolsky, Chief Global Affairs & Legal Officer at Amazon, joined Seattle Mayor Bruce Harrell and local housing developers Great Expectations and Urban Black, to break ground on Acer House, a 121-unit mixed-use community that will provide affordable homes and micro-retail spaces for local entrepreneurs in Seattle’s Central District.Made possible through a $12.6 million low-rate loan from Amazon's housing fund, the development represents an innovative approach to creating affordable housing without traditional funding mechanisms like tax credits or subordinate public loans. Acer House will serve households earning between 40% and 80% of Area Median Income (AMI), and feature six commercial spaces for small local businesses.\"By combining affordable housing with opportunities for local entrepreneurs, this project helps preserve the cultural fabric of the Central District while creating pathways for economic mobility,\" said David Zapolsky. \"We're honored that Amazon's housing fund could help make this vision a reality.”The project leverages the City of Seattle's Multi-Family Tax Exemption program and fulfills the City’s Mandatory Housing Affordability requirements. What makes Amazon's housing investment particularly impactful is that while many public funds specify affordability for 15 to 40 years, Amazon's housing fund ensures affordability for 99 years on most developments.Acer House is a recent example of Amazon's commitment to affordable housing. Since launching with an initial $2 billion commitment in 2021, Amazon has pledged $3.6 billion toward creating or preserving 35,000 affordable homes across the Puget Sound, National Capital, and Nashville regions. In the Puget Sound region alone, Amazon has committed $780 million to support more than 9,900 affordable homes, with $230 million dedicated specifically to Seattle. Amazon contributes $250,000 to the Chinatown International District to establish Ambassadors Program, fosteri Mayor Harrell announces a combined $1 million investment by the City, The Asian American Foundation (TAAF), and Amazon to improve “blocks, businesses, and lives” in the heart of the historic neighborhood.In a shared effort to support safety, economic revitalization, and community well-being in one of Seattle’s most historic and culturally rich neighborhoods, Seattle Mayor Bruce Harrell, TAAF, Amazon, and the Chinatown International District Business Improvement Area (CIDBIA) announced the Chinatown-International District (CID) Ambassadors Program, launching June 1, 2025.The CID Ambassadors Program will train neighborhood ambassadors to serve Japantown, Chinatown, and Little Saigon. These teams will work block by block to assist small businesses, welcome visitors, and foster safer, more vibrant neighborhoods.\"The Chinatown-International District is a vital part of Seattle's cultural and economic fabric and Amazon is proud to support the CID Ambassadors Program as part of our contributions to the revitalization of this important neighborhood,” said David Zapolsky, Chief Global Affairs and Legal Officer of Amazon. “Our investments in Friends of Little Sài Gòn and to revive the CID Night Market reinforce our commitment to fostering economic development, public safety, and cultural celebration.”This also marks the launch of TAAF’s new “TAAF Cities” initiative to spur historic public and private investment to strengthen cities with large Asian American and Pacific Islander (AAPI) populations, of which Amazon is a founding partner. “TAAF Cities” bring together local community, government, business, and nonprofit leaders to develop innovative, community-rooted solutions that promote economic opportunity, safety, and cultural pride. Seattle, a major cultural and economic hub with some of the nation’s oldest and most dynamic AAPI communities, was chosen to kickstart the effort.” Amazon's Josh Hirschland named to Puget Sound Business Journal's 40 Under 40 Puget Sound Business Journal named Josh Hirschland, principal product manager for Amazon Community Impact, a 40 Under 40 honoree for his innovative work addressing food insecurity in the Puget Sound community.As the Amazon Community Delivery program lead, Hirschland applies his logistics expertise to create sustainable, scalable solutions that help get meals directly to the doorsteps of community members in need. Under his guidance, Amazon has offered pro bono delivery for more than 40 million meals to people experiencing food insecurity since 2020—2 million meals across the Puget Sound region.The program launched during the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic when food pantries faced unprecedented demand and many families couldn't leave their homes. Hirschland leveraged Amazon's last-mile delivery network to bridge this gap, transforming an initial pilot into a fully operational program within weeks.Today, Amazon Community Delivery partners with more than 35 organizations in 16 states and expanded to the UK last year. In the Puget Sound region, Amazon collaborates with seven different food banks, including United Way of King County, to serve more than 4,000 families.Amazon believes everyone should have access to healthy food and continues to address the rising rates of food insecurity by partnering with local organizations. Learn more about how Amazon delivers meals to families in need across Puget Sound. Amazon is helping fund 9,900+ affordable homes across the Puget Sound Jared Johnson of Chinatown Preservation & Development talks with Seattleites about Beacon Pacific Village, an affordable housing community with a childcare center and healthy aging and wellness center, situated close to transit, schools, healthcare, and shopping. This multi-generational community received a $3M grant from Amazon’s housing fund, which has fund or preserve more than 9,900 affordable homes across the Puget Sound.Watch the video to learn more. Amazon and the Seattle Storm engage Puget Sound youth through Community Hoops clinics Amazon's continued partnership with the Seattle Storm is supporting the second annual series of Community Hoops clinics—a collaborative effort to bring free, accessible basketball programming to youth across the Puget Sound region.As part of the collaboration, 45 youth participants from Mary’s Place participated in a clinic at the Storm’s Center for Basketball Performance in Seattle’s Interbay neighborhood—many of them experiencing the sport for the first time. A five-week series at the Family First Community Center in Renton welcomed 50 youth weekly from the Benson Hill neighborhood. In Tukwila, 88 students from elementary to high school levels came together for a day of skills-building and community.“The kids had a wonderful time and were so grateful for the experience,” said Zoë Bill, Youth Services Director at Mary’s Place. “Beyond enjoying basketball, they walked away feeling celebrated and a part of something special. Opportunities like this have a lasting impact.”The Seattle Storm’s youth basketball program, Jr. Storm, leads the clinics—putting together drills, offering encouragement, and sharing their love for the game. Their involvement helps create an inspiring environment where kids not only learn basketball fundamentals but also connect with role models.In 2024, Amazon and the Storm hosted eight clinics in partnership with local organizations—reaching hundreds of young athletes and their families. These clinics are just one example of the many ways Amazon invests in local communities across the Puget Sound. We remain committed to supporting neighborhoods where our employees and customers live and work through collaboration with local organizations and programs that create meaningful opportunities for future generations. Amazon gives $2 million to support critical food security initiatives in Seattle region Amazon's investment will help two local food banks expand their capacity to serve community members with essential groceries and services.Amazon provided $2 million to support the growth of two food banks in the Seattle region, with grants of $1 million each to White Center Food Bank and Rainier Valley Food Bank. The donations will help both organizations expand their capacity to provide more community members experiencing food insecurity with vital groceries.White Center Food Bank will use Amazon's grant to support its move to their new, permanent facility in downtown White Center. The location provides improved accessibility through nearby public transportation routes and allows the organization to expand its services.Rainier Valley Food Bank will use its grant to support the renovation and expansion of its facility. The improvements will create a community food hub with that can meet urgent food needs with increased capacity for food storage, a commercial kitchen, and gathering spaces to build community.In addition to supporting the critical work of local food banks, Amazon is committed to addressing food insecurity through multiple initiatives across the communities where we operate. We collaborate with nonprofit organizations, leverage our logistics expertise, and engage our employees to help provide millions of meals to individuals and families in need. Since 2020, our Community Delivery program, which provides free delivery of charitable groceries, has delivered more than 40 million meals with 35 partners in 16 states.Learn more about Amazon's commitment to food security and how we’re helping fight hunger. Amazon's $100 million commitment to affordable housing in the City of Bellevue Amazon is committing $100 million in the City of Bellevue to accelerate the production of affordable housing for low-to-moderate income families. This commitment will support projects throughout Bellevue, with a particular focus on projects supported by the City and on Bellevue's three city-owned transit-oriented sites—giving low-to-moderate income families close proximity to community resources.David Zapolsky, Chief Global Affairs and Legal Officer at Amazon, announced the news at the ground breaking of Spring District, a BRIDGE Housing Community that will bring 234 affordable homes to the City of Bellevue through a transit-oriented development.Learn more about Spring District and Amazon's $100 million commitment to the City of Bellevue. Amazon’s return to office boosts Seattle’s foot traffic and local economy Amazon teams returning to offices are playing a major role in the continued revitalization of downtown Seattle. New data is showing a surge in foot traffic—benefiting local businesses and the broader downtown economy.The Downtown Seattle Association (DSA) reports that weekday worker foot traffic in South Lake Union and the Denny Triangle reached 74% of January 2019 levels, up from 62% the previous year. Across downtown, foot traffic increased 9% from January 2024, reaching 57% of pre-pandemic levels. Nearly 2 million unique visitors came downtown in January, 14% more than the previous year.Amazon’s presence has been a key driver of this uptick. Our 50,000 Seattle employees, along with those in Bellevue and Redmond, are bringing a sense of renewed vibrancy to these communities. Amazon teams are also feeling energized by the shift—excited by the innovation, collaboration, and connection that takes place with teams collaborating in-person.Many local business owners are noticing a welcome increase in weekday customers. “Coffee shops are full, lunch spots are buzzing, and there’s more activity in the area,” noted Downtown Seattle Association President and CEO Jon Scholes.You can read more about Amazon’s return to office plans as we continue to spark energy and innovation on behalf of Amazon’s customers here. Amazon’s commitment to flexible, sustainable commutes in HQ1 At Amazon, we value the connections and opportunities that come from being part of vibrant, downtown communities. By investing in innovative transit options and fostering partnerships, we are able to offer employees a variety of commuter benefits and services to reach their destinations.Across our headquarters cities and in other major metro areas, our employees have access to a variety of reimbursable public transportation costs, free commuter shuttles, and flexible monthly subsidies for commuting expenses.In Puget Sound, we take additional steps to enhance connectivity and help reduce traffic congestion, including:Expanded shuttle services: We increased shuttle routes serving our Puget Sound HQ, including over 30 commuter shuttle routes as far as Mill Creek, North Bend, and West Seattle, intercampus shuttles between our Seattle, Bellevue, and Redmond offices, and shuttles that transport Amazonians to the office from transit hubs and locations around the Puget Sound region.Rideshare: Amazon recently launched a first of its kind Lyft Pass program in Seattle and Bellevue, allowing employees to utilize their Flexible Commuter Subsidy to match rides with fellow Amazonians.Biking: Through their Flexible Commuter Subsidy, Amazon employees can utilize the subsidy to cover costs including: bike leases, bike-share, maintenance, and bike parking. Offices also include bike cages for storage and showers for bikers to get ready for the work day. Sustainable transportation partnerships: Amazon is proud to support local partners that share a commitment to greener commuting options. BellHop, the first all-electric shuttle service in the Pacific Northwest, is one partnership that helps employees replace car trips with a sustainable micro-transit option.Investing in public infrastructure: We work with policymakers and support transit investments that help expand access to public transportation. Amazon advocated for the Move Ahead Washington statewide transportation package which funds over $730 million in traffic reduction projects, including free transit for residents age 18 or younger. Amazon has contributed more than $1.6 million to campaigns that have expanded transit access and invested in and preserved transportation infrastructure. Amazon directly invests in transportation infrastructure, including $10.8 million toward Eastrail and the Grand Connection Crossing.As we move forward, Amazon will continue to explore new ways to enhance our employees’ commute experiences while helping build stronger, greener communities. Amazon supports Memorial Stadium redevelopment in Seattle Amazon is proudly contributing $2 million to the redevelopment of Memorial Stadium, helping to ensure that the iconic venue remains a cherished part of the Seattle community and a vital part of its future. This donation clinched the project’s fundraising by helping it meet its final goal, which included $110 million in public and private funding.Originally built in 1947 to honor Seattle students who lost their lives in World War II, Memorial Stadium has served as a meaningful community hub for decades. From high school football games and graduations, to large-scale events like the Seattle World’s Fair ceremonies, the stadium has played an integral role in the cultural fabric of the city. As the venue has fallen into disrepair, Seattle Public Schools and the City of Seattle partnered with One Roof Stadium Partnership (ORSP) for an ambitious redesign that will result in a modern, state-of-the-art facility. The redevelopment will allow Seattle Public Schools to retain ownership and primary use, while also increasing its capacity and capabilities to host even more events.“Amazon’s donation to the Memorial Stadium demonstrates our long-term commitment to building accessible communities in the Puget Sound,” said David Zapolsky, Senior Vice President, Global Public Policy and General Counsel at Amazon. “We’re thrilled to help transform the stadium into a space for everyone in the community, where neighbors can come together and connect. This reimagined stadium will open doors for more community members and expand upon its legacy, remaining a cherished part of Seattle’s history for years to come.”\"Amazon's generous contribution to the Memorial Stadium redevelopment showcases the power of public-private collaboration in driving systemic change,” said Tracy Myers, Executive Director of King County Play Equity Coalition. \"By modernizing this iconic venue, we're not only preserving Seattle's heritage but also creating a space where young athletes from all backgrounds, particularly those from historically underserved communities, can access the transformative benefits of sports and physical activity.”Scheduled for completion by 2027, the new stadium will increase capacity to over 8,000 and feature additional spaces for athletics, arts, and community events. It will honor the original Memorial Wall, with new elements aligning with the City of Seattle’s goals for equity, sustainability, and community resilience. The reimagined stadium will remain an important centerpiece of Seattle Center—continuing its legacy of creating opportunities for students and the broader community to thrive for years to come.The donation is also part of Amazon’s broader commitment to the Puget Sound region, where we continue to invest in housing, education, food security, and other causes that make a tangible impact in our community. Last year alone, we contributed $68 million to 120 Washington state community partners. Since 2021, we have also committed more than $780 million to help create or preserve affordable housing for nearly 19,000 people through the Amazon Housing Equity Fund. Read more about our commitment to the Puget Sound community in our 2024 Community Impact Report. Amazon invests in vital program to combat food insecurity for Tukwila Schools Amazon has reaffirmed its dedication to supporting communities across the Puget Sound with an investment in Still Waters, a nonprofit organization addressing food insecurity in the Tukwila area. With an $80,000 commitment, Amazon is ensuring that Still Waters’ after-school and weekend food program remains fully funded through the 2026-2027 school year.This vital program currently supports three local elementary schools, reaching 220 students each week. It provides the nutritious meals students need to stay focused, learn effectively, and maintain their overall health. An initial $40,000 donation from Amazon has already secured funding for the 2024-2025 school year, while the remaining $40,000 will co-fund the program’s continuation for the following two school years in collaboration with the City of Tukwila.Local leaders have recognized the importance of this partnership. Tukwila Mayor Thomas McLeod expressed his appreciation, stating, “Still Waters provides critical food security for kids in the Tukwila School District. Amazon’s support ensures that kids will have healthy meals and not go hungry.”For Still Waters, Amazon’s commitment means the continuation of a program that many families rely on. Abigail Tabason, Executive Director of Still Waters, shared her gratitude, saying, “At Still Waters, our team believes in order to achieve food and educational equity in Tukwila Schools, all students deserve access to nutrition. We are so grateful for the support from Amazon and the City of Tukwila. This will ensure there is no interruption of this program that many Tukwila families rely on.”The impact of Amazon’s contributions extends well beyond this latest effort. Since 2021, the company has donated over 8 million pounds of food in Washington State and provided more than $2.9 million to local organizations in the Puget Sound region. These investments are part of Amazon’s broader commitment to addressing food insecurity and supporting individuals and families in need.“Amazon has been deeply connected to the Puget Sound region for more than 30 years,” said Taylor Hoang, Senior Manager for Community Engagement at Amazon. “We remain committed to taking a hands-on approach to work with nonprofits and community partners to find creative solutions for some of our region’s most pressing needs. We’re proud to be able to support organizations like Still Waters in their critical work to help combat food insecurity.” Amazon’s Winter Wonderland brings holiday cheer to Puget Sound families On Saturday, December 7, Amazon hosted its second annual Winter Wonderland, an event designed to bring holiday joy to underserved children and families in the Puget Sound region.Over 1,000 children and their families gathered at the Amazon Meeting Center in Seattle for a day filled with holiday magic. Each child had the chance to select toys, books, and other goodies while creating special memories with their families. Parents and guardians also received a $50 gift credit per child to shop on Amazon.com, with purchases delivered directly to their homes—making the holidays brighter and more convenient.Winter Wonderland welcomed families through partnerships with local nonprofits including Byrd Barr Place, El Centro de la Raza, Treehouse, Rainier Athletes, and Wellspring Family Services. Families also enjoyed photo opportunities, seasonal crafts, refreshments, and live holiday music. Adding to the excitement, former Seattle Seahawks players along with Seahawks mascots and dancers, made special appearances throughout the day.Amazon’s Senior Vice President of Global Public Policy, David Zapolsky, had the opportunity to sit down with KING 5’s New Day Northwest to chat about Winter Wonderland while reflecting on how Amazon has cultivated a \"culture of giving.” This comes to life through hundreds of thousands of volunteer hours from our teams, and investments that uplift communities where our customers and employees live and work.At Winter Wonderland, 300 Amazon volunteers staffed the toy shop, assisted at online shopping stations, led craft activities, and even dressed up as holiday mascots to spread festive cheer. Amazon expands partnerships with Puget Sound food banks to deliver groceries to neighbors in need undefined Amazon and the Gary Sinise Foundation team up to support Washington state first responders In honor of National First Responders Day, observed on October 28th, Amazon once again joined forces with the Gary Sinise Foundation (GSF)—a leader in supporting first responders, veterans, and their families—to celebrate and support those who put their lives on the line for their communities.Amazon and GSF donated more than $200,000 in new equipment to fire departments across Washington state. This equipment is enhancing first responders’ ability to perform their lifesaving work safely and effectively.Amazon and GSF visited local fire departments to present the grants and serve meals to first responder teams as a small token of gratitude. Spokane County Fire District 10, Franklin County Fire District 3, Everett Fire Department and West Pierce Fire & Rescue were all chosen as this partnership prioritizes under-resourced fire departments with larger numbers of volunteer firefighters. The fire departments used the grants to purchase fire safety equipment, such as educational inflatables, bail out kits and extrication equipment, critical for enabling firefighters to quickly escape compromised structures and remove individuals from trapped vehicles or buildings.“Through our incredible three-year partnership, Amazon has displayed immense compassion for our mission and a commitment to delivering direct, meaningful impact to the heroes of our communities—the first responders who put their lives on the line every day to keep us safe,” said Chrissy Kreisel, senior director of corporate and foundation relations at the Gary Sinise Foundation.GSF was established in 2011 under the philanthropic direction of award-winning actor and humanitarian Gary Sinise, who has been a staunch supporter of veterans, first responders and defenders for more than 40 years. From providing equipment to hosting events like GSF’s healing retreats for families of fallen military and first responder heroes, Amazon is proud to be able to make a tangible difference. Community Impact Report: Amazon showcases commitment to Puget Sound Region At Amazon, we live our mission of making our customers’ lives better and easier every day. We also bring this innovative, inventive spirit to empower the communities we call home, using our scale and abilities to take on some of society’s most pressing challenges. Working side-by-side with community partners, we’re increasing access to affordable housing and alleviating hunger. We’re strengthening education, promoting literacy, and helping people gain skills for the future.Our most recent Community Impact Report showcases the progress we’re making and the positive impact we believe we’re having on everyday life for our Puget Sound neighbors. Highlights include:We contributed $68 million last year in support of more than 120 community partners dedicated to making the Puget Sound region a better place to live. Through initiatives large and small, our partners’ work has an indelible impact—increasing access to cultural events, providing free gifts for the holiday season, supporting a free electric shuttle service, and addressing the needs of people experiencing homelessness.Since launching the Amazon Housing Equity Fund in 2021, we have committed over $780 million to help create or preserve affordable housing for more than 18,900 low- to moderate-income individuals so they can live in the communities they serve and love. This is especially important for first responders, teachers, nurses, and service industry employees who are getting priced out of expensive metro areas like ours.We’ve donated more than 8 million meals to local organizations like Food Lifeline, FareStart, and Ballard Food Bank, helping them address food insecurity and hunger across the region. And we have used our logistics network to deliver more than 2 million meals to families, making it easier for them to access the food they need.We continue to invest in programs to help children in grades K-12, higher-education students, and adult learners unlock their potential. By focusing on critical digital skills, we prepare these individuals for the jobs of the future. Through Amazon Future Engineer we delivered 146,000 student hours of STEM education, literacy, and career exploration courses across the Puget Sound. And over 58,000 learners have accessed free training and education at the AWS Skills Center in Seattle.These are just a few of the ways that we are giving back to the region that we call home. We are committed to supporting and collaborating with our neighbors and communities throughout the Puget Sound Region. We know that—working together—we can build an even brighter future.Learn more in our Community Impact Report for the Puget Sound region. Amazon celebrates the opening of Overlook Walk and Revitalization of Seattle’s Waterfront Park Earlier this month, Amazon team members joined City of Seattle Mayor Bruce Harrell, the Office of the Waterfront, and other community leaders to celebrate the much-anticipated opening of Seattle’s new Overlook Walk.Located where the Alaskan Way Viaduct once stood, Overlook Walk creates the first ever pedestrian connection between Seattle’s downtown and waterfront—bridging a nearly 100-foot gap between Pike Place Market and the Waterfront Park Promenade adjacent to Elliott Bay. Visitors and residents will enjoy ample seating, stunning views, a new children’s play area, as well as landscaping that reflects the cultural heritage and natural environment of the Pacific Northwest.“Seattle’s latest iconic urban element, Overlook Walk, is joining the roster of recognizable, dynamic Seattle landmarks—as a symbol of our city’s identity,” said Mayor Harrell during the opening ceremony.As part of Amazon’s continued commitment to invest in the community for its neighbors across the Puget Sound region, the company provided $7 million in funding to the revitalization of Seattle’s Waterfront Park.\"Amazon is thrilled to have played a part in bringing Overlook Walk to life, which shows our dedication to building a more accessible and connected community,\" said David Zapolsky, Senior Vice President of Global Public Policy and General Counsel at Amazon. \"This new pathway seamlessly connects downtown, Pike Place Market, and the waterfront, celebrating Seattle’s innovative spirit, natural beauty, and the power of collaboration.\" Overlook Walk marks the near completion of a decades-long Waterfront Park effort, to fully open to the public in 2025. Amazon signs agreement to bring new nuclear energy to Washington As the energy needs of our business and customers continue to grow, we’re continuing to invest in renewables while also finding additional sources of carbon-free energy that can both help power our operations and bring new sources of energy to the grid. Nuclear power is one part of that mix—it can be brought online at scale, and has a decades-long record of providing a reliable source of safe carbon-free energy for communities around the world.We recently announced that we’ve signed several agreements to support the development of nuclear energy projects—including enabling the construction of several new Small Modular Reactors (SMRs). SMRs are an advanced kind of nuclear reactor with a smaller physical footprint, allowing them to be built closer to the grid. They also have faster build times than traditional reactors, allowing them to come online sooner.In Washington, our agreement with Energy Northwest, a consortium of state public utilities, will enable the development of four advanced SMRs. The reactors will be constructed, owned and operated by Energy Northwest, and are expected to generate roughly 320 megawatts (MW) of capacity for the first phase of the project, with the option to increase to 960 MW total—enough to power the equivalent of more than 770,000 U.S. homes. These projects will help meet the forecasted energy needs of the Pacific Northwest beginning in the early 2030s and is expected to support up to 1,000 temporary construction jobs and as many as 100+ permanent jobs once fully operational, according to Energy Northwest. Amazon is hiring 5,000 people across Washington as it heads into the holiday season As the 2024 holiday shopping season approaches, Amazon announced that it’s hiring for 250,000 full-time, part-time, and seasonal roles across the U.S., including 5,000 positions in Washington, including 4,000 in the Seattle-Tacoma-Bellevue region.Seasonal Amazon jobs offer competitive pay, flexible schedules, and the opportunity to be part of the bustling retail industry during its busiest time of year. Many of these seasonal roles also come with the potential to transition into regular, full-time employment after the holidays. All seasonal employees earn at least $18 per hour and have access to benefits like health care from the first day on the job.We’re also looking forward to welcoming back many familiar faces—each season we see many individuals come back year after year to be a part of the holiday season here at Amazon. In fact, nearly one-third of people who have come to work at Amazon as a holiday hire return. Whatever someone’s personal circumstances may be, Amazon has an opportunity for them.A great example of an employee who found a long-term career at Amazon is Liv Uribe, currently based out of Washington state. She started in a seasonal role at the PHX5 fulfillment center in 2011 and, after nine promotions, is now a Senior Regional Human Recourses Manager. “The seasonal position allowed me to get my foot in the door at Amazon,” said Liv. “I loved the company and the people I worked with, and saw the opportunities to grow, so I pursued a full-time role after that first holiday season.\"Last month we also announced our largest annual wage increase ever for our hourly associates. Members of our front-line team will be received at least an additional $1.50 per hour starting September 29, which brings their average base wage to more than $23 per hour in Washington, and more than $30 in total compensation including elected benefits. Amazon expands partnerships with Puget Sound food banks to deliver groceries to neighbors in need Every Monday, Amazon Flex drivers arrive at West Seattle Food Bank to pick up boxes of healthy food to deliver them to families and individuals in need, for free. In partnership with local nonprofit United Way of King County (UWKC), and building on their existing delivery program with West Seattle Food Bank, Amazon recently expanded our Community Delivery program to support food banks in the University District, Auburn, Covington, and Kent. People who receive these deliveries have signed up through the Home Grocery Delivery Program facilitated by UWKC, which partners with Amazon and local food banks to donate and deliver vital groceries right to their doorstep.“Amazon makes it possible to think about this program long-term,” said Sara Seelmeyer, food security program manager, UWKC. “The home delivery program began during the pandemic and continues because it has proven to be invaluable in serving those most at-risk in our community, but it is not sustainable without Amazon's support. Currently, Amazon donates this service for roughly 900 deliveries per week.”Amazon believes everyone should have access to healthy food and is addressing hunger with local partners by tapping into the company’s technology and delivery network where access to food can be a struggle. So far, Amazon’s Community Delivery program has delivered over 1.8 million meals to more than 4,000 families across Puget Sound.Learn more about Amazon Community Delivery and how we’re helping fight hunger across the U.S. Amazon Sponsors Return of the Night Market to Seattle’s Chinatown-International District The annual Night Market in Seattle's historic Chinatown-International District returned on Saturday, September 21st, following Amazon's $250,000 commitment to bring the food and culture street festival back for the next three years. As part of the diverse fabric that makes up the Puget Sound community, Amazon was proud to help bring back this unique local event.Hosted by the Chinatown-International District Business Improvement Area (CIDBIA), the 2024 Night Market was a lively celebration of culture, food, and community, drawing over 30,000 visitors from across the region and providing a boost in foot traffic to the district. More than 100 vendors participated, with a mix of traditional and modern food and beverage offerings, as well as local artisans selling handmade goods. Attendees also enjoyed live music, dancing, martial arts demonstrations, and other cultural performances throughout the evening.In celebration of the market’s return, and as part of its commitment to addressing food insecurity, Amazon partnered with Uwajimaya grocery stores to donate 75,000 pounds of food to local food banks. Amazon will contribute 50,000 pounds of food to organizations including Asian Counseling and Referral Services (ACRS) and Hopelink. Uwajimaya has launched an in-store donation campaign to fund the additional 25,000 pounds of food.“At Uwajimaya, we’ve been a proud part of this community since 1928, and we believe in the strength of community and the importance of giving back,” said Denise Moriguchi, President and CEO of Uwajimaya. “Partnering with Amazon on this food drive allows us to leverage our shared commitment to supporting local families, especially during the back-to-school season when the need for nutritious meals is crucial. By working together, we’re able to make a lasting impact, both by providing essential resources and by strengthening the cultural fabric of the neighborhoods we’ve served for nearly a century.” Amazon teams up to clean Seattle’s parks: 300 volunteers make an impact On September 7, more than 300 Amazon employees and their families participated in Amazon's fourth Parks Cleanup in partnership with Seattle Parks Foundation and Seattle Parks and Recreation (SPR). Volunteers at Green Lake Park, Meadowbrook Park, Seward Park, and Genesee Park/Playfield spread 110 yards of mulch, fertilized 80 trees, removed 900 square feet of invasive weeds, and cleaned up litter from 300 feet of shoreline.At Seward Park, Amazon’s Vice President of Sustainability, Kara Hurst, joined the cleanup alongside AP Diaz, Superintendent of Seattle Parks and Recreation, and Kristen Milliron, COO of Seattle Parks Foundation. Together, they highlighted the importance of maintaining green spaces for the community. Amazon and Bumbershoot partnership surpasses food donation goal Over Labor Day weekend, more than 30,000 fans gathered at Seattle Center for the annual Bumbershoot Arts & Music Festival. The event brought people together to enjoy and experience an eclectic mix of music, multimedia art, dance, performances, and food from 29 local restaurants.As part of this year’s festival, Amazon teamed up with Bumbershoot for \"Bumbershoot Gives Back: Powered by Amazon,\" a community-driven initiative aimed to address food insecurity in the region. Together, we surpassed our goal of donating 90,000 pounds of food, ultimately providing 127,000 pounds to food banks across the Puget Sound. Additionally, Conscious Alliance, a nonprofit hunger relief organization, organized a virtual food drive where supporters received a limited-edition Bumbershoot poster in exchange for donations that provided meals toward the program.Throughout the summer, Bumbershoot and Amazon mobilized more than 2,000 volunteers who contributed more than 10,000 hours of service to 15 local organizations, including food banks and community gardens. These volunteers dedicated time to combat food insecurity by delivering groceries, sorting food, and harvesting gardens, earning free two-day festival tickets for their efforts.Amazon takes a hands-on approach to address key issues like hunger in the communities in which we live and work. By investing in and collaborating with community partners, we’re providing a direct and positive impact on the lives of those in need. Amazon joins the fun at the 18th annual SLU Block Party Last month, 6,500 people came out to enjoy the fun and festivities at the 18th annual South Lake Union (SLU) Block Party, coming together to support local businesses and community organizations in celebration of the neighborhood’s culture and history.This year’s SLU Block Party was a lively affair, with 34 community booths, seven food trucks, and a variety of activities, showcasing what makes SLU special. From the energetic performances by Pacific Northwest artists Little Lies, Thief Motif, and Nite Wave, to a yoga session led by Core Power Yoga, there was something for everyone. Guests of all ages also participated in the “Street Scramble” neighborhood scavenger hunt and played lawn games.Amazon’s role in the SLU Block Party goes beyond sponsorship. We’re proud to support the diverse mix of businesses, nonprofits, and community-based organizations that call SLU home. This year, several of our own retail tenants, including the Gage Academy of Arts—a nonprofit art school that recently relocated to Amazon’s SLU campus—Kathmandu, and Bright Horizons, joined in the festivities. The Block Party provided these and other local businesses with an opportunity to further connect with the community and strengthen the neighborhood’s ties.“Our mission has always been to create vibrant, 18-hour districts that benefit both Amazonians and the surrounding neighborhood,” said Shannon Loew, vice president of Global Real Estate & Facilities, Amazon. “The SLU Block Party is a perfect example of how we bring this mission to life, fostering a lively and connected community that supports a diverse mix of businesses, arts, and cultural organizations.”Amazon remains committed to supporting the people and organizations that make the Puget Sound region a dynamic place to live and work. Events like the Block Party remind us that our greatest achievements come from working together, building connections, and creating spaces where everyone feels welcome.We’re already looking forward to next year’s Block Party and can’t wait to see how the community continues to thrive. Amazon Fresh cultivates healthy eating habits and educational opportunities across the Puget Sound Amazon Fresh has teamed up with Whole Foods Market Foundation by donating $250,000 to fund 83 school gardens across the country, including local schools across the Puget Sound region. This initiative aligns with Amazon Fresh's long-term vision of positively impacting local communities by promoting access to healthy food choices and educational initiatives.Among the beneficiaries are Halilits Elementary on Bainbridge Island, Morningside Academy, Graham Hill Elementary School, and Technology Access Foundation in Seattle, as well as Wildwood Elementary in Federal Way and Hazelwood Elementary School in Newcastle. These schools will receive Whole Kids Garden Grants provided by Amazon Fresh, enabling them to establish or enhance their edible gardens and engage students in hands-on learning about growing and enjoying fresh produce.\"We're thrilled to support these local schools and their efforts to cultivate a love for nutritious foods among students,\" said Stockton Young, regional manager for Amazon Fresh in the Puget Sound region. \"By providing access to edible gardens, we aim to inspire a new generation of kids to develop an understanding of where their food comes from.\"\"When kids have a hand in growing fruits and vegetables, they are more likely to try them, enjoy them, and inspire other kids to do the same. Additionally, kids who grow up enjoying fruits and vegetables are more likely to continue eating them through adulthood,\" said Carol Medeiros, executive director of Whole Foods Market Foundation. \"We’re so grateful to Amazon Fresh whose generous donation in the Whole Kids Garden Grant program this year will allow more students to get a jumpstart on a lifetime of enjoying healthy food.\"Through this collaboration, Amazon Fresh reinforces its commitment to investing in the well-being of local communities. By nurturing a love for fresh produce and sustainable practices from an early age, the company hopes to inspire positive change and contribute to a healthier, more sustainable future for all. First newly-constructed affordable housing community to receive Amazon funding in King County opens Since the Amazon Housing Equity Fund (HEF) launched in January 2021, we’ve dedicated $3.6 billion to help fund more than 35,000 affordable homes across the Puget Sound region, the Arlington, Virginia/D.C. region, and Nashville, Tennessee. Today, Amazon celebrated the opening of Grata at Totem Lake―the first newly-constructed affordable housing community to receive HEF funding in King County. There is a great need for more affordable housing across the Puget Sound―this is especially true on the east side of Lake Washington. Grata at Totem Lake will help address this need with 125 units, ranging from studios to 4-bedroom apartments, for residents earing less than 60% of the county’s area median income (AMI), and with 88 of these homes reserved for residents earning under 50% AMI. Grata at Totem Lake will also contribute to residents’ quality of life by providing easy access to nearby transit, groceries and retail, healthcare, schools and other resources. The building is also energy-efficient and includes rooftop solar arrays, green-rated appliances in each unit, a design that reduces energy usage, and EV-charging stations.TWG Development led the development of Grata at Totem Lake, which received a $12.5 million low-rate loan from the HEF, along with funding from A Regional Coalition for Housing (ARCH), the Washington State Housing Finance Commission, and Key Bank. This new development illustrates what can happen when the private sector, public sector, and community-based organizations collaborate to address the need for more affordable housing.Of the $3.6 billion that Amazon has dedicated to help create or preserve affordable housing through Housing Equity Fund, $2.2 billion has been committed to help create or preserve more than 21,000 affordable homes so far, including over $780 million to help create or preserve more than 8,600 affordable homes across the Puget Sound. Over 80% of the affordable housing developments funded by Amazon have been in partnership with local, state, and federal government, and we plan to build on these partnerships to help create more communities like Grata at Totem Lake. Learn more about Amazon's Housing Equity Fund and what Amazon is doing in the Puget Sound region for our employees, neighbors, and the community. Amazon teams with Bumbershoot to provide free tickets while fighting hunger As the community gears up for the Bumbershoot arts and music festival later this summer, Amazon has partnered with Bumbershoot on the “Bumbershoot Gives Back” initiative, an effort to support food security for our neighbors in the region. The collaboration will deliver 90,000 pounds of food to food banks in the region, while providing fans with meaningful ways to serve the community and opportunities to receive Bumbershoot festival tickets in the process. To kick off the program, Amazon donated 30,000 pounds of food to the Asian Counseling & Referral Services’ (ACRS) food bank in Seattle’s Chinatown-International District. Amazon volunteers helped unload the donated food that will support ACRS’ food security programs across the Puget Sound.To encourage community participation, Amazon’s support will make 2,000 two-day festival tickets available for free to those who volunteer their time with select local nonprofits tackling food insecurity across the Puget Sound. There will also be limited-edition posters available for sale, with each purchase providing 80 meals to contribute to the program.The collaboration is part of a larger effort to address the issue of food insecurity in Western Washington, which impacts over 1.5 million of our neighbors.“We’re proud of our investments across Seattle and the Puget Sound region over the last 30 years, and as we continue to grow, so does our responsibility to the places where we live and work,” said David Zapolsky, Senior Vice President, Global Public Policy and General Counsel for Amazon. “Amazon is committed to working with our partners to find creative solutions that empower our community. This year, we’re excited to be working with an iconic local institution like Bumbershoot to have our sponsorship support critical needs in the region. Through this work, our impact will result in thousands of volunteers mobilizing across the Puget Sound while we infuse local food banks with 90,000 pounds of food.”Amazon’s work to tackle food insecurity nationwide has resulted in more than 88 million pounds of food donated to food banks, pantries, kitchens, and shelters in the U.S. since 2021, with more than seven million pounds donated within Washington state. In addition, more than $2.9 million has been donated to local organizations to support individuals and families experiencing food insecurity since 2021 in the Puget Sound.Bumbershoot will return to Seattle Center this Labor Day Weekend. Learn more about Bumbershoot and how to sign up for a volunteer opportunity at a community-based organization. Amazon celebrates Seafair and military veterans This year marks the 75th anniversary of Seafair, the biggest summer celebration in the Pacific Northwest. Amazon is honored to sponsor the annual Fourth of July fireworks at Gas Works Park for the fourth year in a row.The fireworks are both a beloved local family tradition, as well as an opportunity for our Puget Sound community to honor veterans while celebrating the contributions and sacrifices of military families everywhere. This milestone 75th anniversary event coincides with Amazon’s celebration of surpassing our goal to hire 100,000 veterans and military spouses this year. In support of veterans and their families, Amazon offers a broad range of programs built to address challenges specific to our military community, including Warriors@, an affinity group that helps smooth the transition to civilian careers.Whether joining as an hourly or a salaried employee, employees also receive comprehensive benefits, including health care from Day 1 on the job and a wide range of career growth and training programs.David Zapolsky, Amazon’s senior vice president of Global Public Policy and General Counsel, sat down with Seattle’s New Day Northwest morning show to share why Amazon’s employment opportunities and alignment of skills represent a great workplace for veterans.Also joining Zapolsky and host Amity Addrisi was Ansel Doub, a Navy veteran and Amazon site leader who has been with Amazon for nearly 12 years. Doub shared how he’s benefitted from mentorship and being welcomed by the military community within Amazon—as well as how this represents a full circle moment for him, as he participated in Seafair while on a Naval ship back in 2004.Following a full day of July Fourth fun at Gas Works Park and the South Lake Union viewing areas, Amazon’s own Alexa also joined the festivities this year, with hosts calling on Alexa to start the fireworks during the live televised broadcast.Learn more about Seafair. Amazon adds $1.4 billion to the Housing Equity Fund to create and preserve more affordable homes in the Puget We’re now extending our commitment to the Amazon Housing Equity Fund with an additional $1.4 billion in funding to create and preserve 14,000 more homes in three of its hometown communities—Washington State's Puget Sound region; the Arlington, Virginia/National Capital region; and Nashville, Tennessee. This brings the total to more than $3.6 billion to fund more than 35,000 affordable homes across these three regions. The extension of the Housing Equity Fund underscores our commitment to affordable housing and to helping thousands of families live closer to where they work or near transportation hubs, removing a major barrier to success. To date, Amazon's Housing Equity Fund has funded a total of 8,600+ homes across the Puget Sound region and increased affordable housing stock by 30% in Bellevue, WA.Learn more about this extension of the Housing Equity Fund and how the Amazon Housing Equity Fund is helping create affordable housing across communities that Amazon calls home. Amazon volunteers join in with \"Keep Bellevue Beautiful\" initiative Last week, Amazon employees participated in the City of Bellevue’s \"Keep Bellevue Beautiful\" volunteer event. In collaboration with the City of Bellevue, 100 Amazonians took to the streets for a massive trash pickup effort. Volunteers worked diligently to clean up and beautify various areas around downtown. This effort is a testament to our dedication to fostering safe, clean, and vibrant communities.\"Amazon’s involvement sets a high standard for corporate social responsibility,” said Mayor Lynne Robinson of Bellevue. “We are grateful for their continued support and dedication to our city. Their efforts during this event not only enhance the beauty of Bellevue, but also strengthen the bonds within our community.\"Amazon has been a key player in the \"Keep Bellevue Beautiful\" program since its launch in August 2023, standing out as the only large-scale corporate participant to date. This involvement underscores our role as a leading community partner, committed to making a positive difference in the places where our employees live and work.\"We are proud to be a part of the ‘Keep Bellevue Beautiful’ initiative,” said Luli Chaluleu, vice president of Talent Acquisition for Worldwide Amazon Stores. “Our volunteers' hard work reflects Amazon’s core values of community engagement and environmental responsibility. We look forward to continuing our partnership with the City of Bellevue to create a better future for everyone.\"“Keep Bellevue Beautiful,” was just one event during Amazon’s Global Month of Volunteering, a company-wide initiative to engage in volunteering across our many global communities, demonstrating the company's strong commitment to community involvement and environmental stewardship. Amazon is dedicated to supporting local community causes. Since 2021, we have donated over $244 million to local charities and nonprofit organizations, establishing ourselves as the region's largest corporate contributor. Additionally, through the Amazon Housing Equity Fund, we have invested $720 million to preserve and create over 8,000 affordable homes across the Puget Sound region. Amazon, named amongst the region's top corporate philanthropists, is tackling the most pressing community iss Amazon was recognized by the Puget Sound Business Journal for a third year in a row. The company’s $244M community investment over the past three years—$68 million in 2023—is creating measurable and positive impacts across the region.According to the Building Industry Association of Washington, 84% of families in Washington state cannot afford to buy a home. Low- and middle-income households are disproportionately affected as they often struggle to afford basic necessities because housing costs consume more than 30% of their monthly income. At the same time, according to Food Lifeline, the region saw a 63% increase in visits to local food banks in 2023.Alongside our local partners, Amazon is providing immediate support to address the region’s most pressing issues, while investing in long-term solutions that can be sustained over time. For example, a partnership with Mary’s Place provided more than 160,000 bed nights at the organization’s shelter inside Amazon’s Seattle headquarters to families experiencing homelessness. Amazon’s Housing Equity Fund (HEF) was started to preserve existing, and create newer, affordable homes near modes of transit, schools, and health care facilities. HEF has already helped fund more than 8,000 affordable homes and supported over 17,000 low-to-moderate-income residents.Amazon is also using its unique logistics capabilities to reduce hunger. In 2023, Amazon delivered more than 500,000 pounds of food to nonprofits in the Puget Sound region, and has donated more than 7 million pounds of food to food banks, pantries, kitchens, and shelters across Washington state since 2021.While we are thankful to our partners for the progress we’ve made together in our hometown, there is more work to be done. Amazon will continue to invest in issues that matter most to our neighbors, and collaborate with local organizations, to ensure an even brighter, more equitable tomorrow.Learn more about the ways Amazon is increasing access to housing, food, and education across the Puget Sound region in our 2023 Community Impact Report. Amazon celebrates opening of new 2 Line light rail line with Bellevue leaders Over the past few years, Amazon has continued to share news of our investments and growth across Puget Sound headquarters. Amazon continues to make progress towards our commitment to bring 25,000 jobs to Bellevue, and we’ve opened several new offices across the area, from Sonic and Dynamo in Bellevue, to Silverstone in Redmond. We also know that while jobs and workspaces are important, Amazon is equally as committed to ensuring our growth is sustainable and that we’re investing in the right infrastructure to support our hiring. As a result of this work, we’re excited to celebrate the opening of the East Link Extension, officially known as the 2 Line, that will serve eight new stations from South Bellevue to Redmond, further connecting cities and neighborhoods across the Eastside.“Our downtown Bellevue campus was designed around the connectivity that mass transit provides, both for the community and the businesses that are growing here,\" said David Zapolsky, senior vice president, Global Public Policy and General Counsel at Amazon. “Not only will this investment provide easier access to downtown Bellevue for our 12,000 Amazon employees in the city, but as we continue to invest in affordable housing with access to nearby public transit through the Amazon Housing Equity Fund, the 2 Line will also begin to open up new opportunities for the broader community.”As part of the 2 Line opening and Amazon’s hands-on approach to supporting mass transit, we will provide up to 15,000 pre-loaded ORCA cards to members of the community to promote ridership and support increased public transit access for the community.Our commitment to Bellevue goes beyond the jobs we create, the investment we bring, and the infrastructure we support. Amazon continues to work closely with the community and its leaders to ensure that we’re helping to address the issues that matter most to them. This includes contributing over $270 million to create and preserve 1,700 affordable homes on the Eastside through Amazon’s Housing Equity Fund; donating nearly $3 million to Bellevue LifeSpring through the Right Now Needs Fund to help address students’ basic and immediate needs like food, shelter, clothing, and school supplies so students arrive at school prepared to learn; and direct funding and support to over 100 non-profits on the Eastside. Amazon funds the revitalization of Seattle local beach park Amazon celebrated the grand re-opening of Seattle’s Be'er Sheva Park on Saturday, April 13, 2024. The park has undergone significant renovations, and Amazon is proud to provide support to close the funding gap needed to complete the project.“Amazon’s generous, transformational support helped the Rainier Beach community bring the vision for this project to fruition,” said Rebecca Bear, president and CEO, Seattle Parks Foundation. “Be’er Sheva Park now has beach access, restored salmon habitat, a cultural stage, and much needed picnic areas. The park now better reflects the needs of the neighborhood and will support a thriving community in Rainier Beach.”The improvements, resulting from a public-private partnership led by Seattle Parks and Recreation, Rainier Beach Link2Lake, and the Seattle Parks Foundation, includes art installations, a boardwalk with lighted paths, and a stage for cultural performances, along with new access to the beach for members of the local community. Located in southeast Seattle, the 25.5-acre park on the shores of Lake Washington is the perfect place to visit on a summer day. With stunning views of the Cascade Mountains, it is a popular destination for picnics, family gatherings, yoga classes, and more.“The Rainier Beach community is one of Seattle’s most dynamic and diverse neighborhoods. Amazon remains committed to providing solutions for causes that drive meaningful change for our neighbors across the Puget Sound region,” said Taylor Hoang, senior community engagement manager at Amazon. “The re-opening of this park brings the community access to native plants, artwork, walkways, and beach access just in time to enjoy this spring and summer.” With support from Amazon, 445 homes are staying affordable in southeast Seattle Amazon’s Housing Equity Fund is providing $11.8 million+ in below-market loans, grants to preserve these affordable homes for current and future residents.Access to affordable housing is one of the greatest challenges facing low to moderate-income households, including residents in communities across the Puget Sound region where Amazon has a strong presence.Preservation of existing affordable housing is one of the most effective ways to ensure affordability, as it helps keep people in their homes who might otherwise be priced out, and helps ensure homes that are affordable today stay affordable for the long-term.In cities like Seattle, however, where the cost of residential real estate continues to climb, many naturally occurring affordable rental units (homes that have, historically, been affordable to renters) are at risk of being sold and converted to market-rate homes, putting them out of reach for current and future residents in need of affordable housing.For these reasons, The Amazon Housing Equity Fund (HEF) prioritizes efforts to preserve (and improve) existing affordable housing, making it possible for low to moderate-income residents to stay in their communities. Two existing affordable Seattle apartment communities, the Claremont and Lake Washington Apartments, have been kept affordable by SouthEast Effective Development (SEED), a local nonprofit dedicated to improving the quality of life in Southeast Seattle through housing, economic development and the arts. With $11.8 million in financial support from HEF, SEED was able to ensure these 445 homes stay affordable for the next 99 years.“Southeast Seattle is facing some of the greatest displacement pressures in Washington State. The Claremont and Lake Washington Apartments will provide a critical resource for residents in need of quality, affordable housing. We’re thankful for Amazon’s support, which is helping us keep these homes affordable today and for future generations,” said Michael Seiwerath, Executive Director, SEED.The Claremont, located in Seattle’s Mt. Baker neighborhood, consists of 68 affordable homes for families earning between 50% to 80% of the Area Median Income (AMI). Lake Washington Apartments, located in Seattle’s Rainier Beach neighborhood (and within walking distance of Lake Washington), consists of 377 affordable homes for families earning between 30% to 60% AMI. Both of these apartment communities are in highly desirable neighborhoods—close to mass transit, schools, employment centers, and other neighborhood amenities.“When we keep existing homes affordable, we help foster greater equity across our communities,” said Senthil Sankaran, Managing Principal, Amazon Housing Equity Fund. “By preserving the affordability of the Claremont and Lake Washington Apartments, HEF and SEED are helping current and future residents have access to high-quality affordable homes and surrounding resources that contribute to a better quality of life.”Learn more about The Amazon Housing Equity Fund. Continuing to expand our Puget Sound headquarters in Bellevue Over the last few months we’ve shared some exciting updates about the expansion of our Puget Sound headquarters in Bellevue. In December, we announced that 1,000 employees moved into the first half of Sonic, also known as Tower 555. Then in February, we celebrated the opening of our next building, Dynamo, bringing an additional 1,500 employees to Bellevue, Washington. And today we’re excited to share that we are continuing construction on the remaining 22 floors at Sonic, bringing online an additional 500,000 square feet of collaborative workspace in Bellevue.
